_meta.description: Strategies 6–11 with long definitions added (v1.3). This is chunk 02 of the full set.

speaking: Students will orally {function} {content_focus} using {supports} (e.g., {frames}), producing {product} that meets {criteria}.
Students will discuss {content_focus} in pairs, using {frames} to {function} and incorporate {target_vocab}.
listening: Students will comprehend {input_type} on {content_focus} and demonstrate understanding by {response_mode} using {supports}.
Students will follow {procedural_input} and {function} (e.g., sequence, identify) the steps for {content_focus}.
reading: Students will read {text_type} about {content_focus} and {function} (e.g., identify, infer) key ideas using {strategy} and {target_vocab}.
Students will use {strategy} (e.g., cognates, text features) to make meaning from {text_type} about {content_focus}.
writing: Students will write a {genre} to {purpose} about {content_focus}, using {frames}/{target_vocab} and {language_features}.
Students will revise a short paragraph to {function} (e.g., explain, justify) understanding of {content_focus} using {criteria}.

speaking+listening: In structured talk, students will {function} about {content_focus}, using {frames} while peers listen and {response_mode}.
reading+writing: Students will read {text_type} on {content_focus} and write a {genre} that {purpose}, using {target_vocab} and {criteria}.
all: Through {framework}, students will listen to {input_type}, discuss {content_focus}, read {text_type}, and write a {genre} that {purpose}, with {supports}.

strategies.core_principle: Compare English and L1 structures to address predictable errors and build awareness.
implementation_steps: Present side-by-side examples; state rules in both languages.
Practice with translation/correction tasks; reference during feedback.
source_docs: Bilingual Teaching Strategies Reference Guide - ChatGPT.docx
Bilingual Teaching Strategies Research Guide - Gemini.docx

definitions.short_en: Teaching students to compare L1 and English structures to anticipate challenges and highlight transferable knowledge.
definitions.long_en: Contrastive Analysis is the systematic comparison of linguistic structures in students’ L1 and in English to highlight similarities and differences. Teachers guide students to notice phonological, morphological, syntactic, or pragmatic features that transfer positively (e.g., cognates, similar word order) and those that may cause errors (e.g., false cognates, article use). In practice, teachers present side‑by‑side examples, chart similarities, and explicitly point out potential pitfalls. Students practice transforming sentences across languages and reflect on how knowledge of L1 helps anticipate English patterns. This fosters metalinguistic awareness and prevents fossilization of common errors. Contrastive analysis is especially effective when introducing complex grammar, writing conventions, or academic registers. It should not devolve into prescriptive ‘avoid mistakes’ lists; instead, it should celebrate cross‑linguistic resources while clarifying non‑equivalences.
look_fors: Side‑by‑side L1–English examples on board or chart
Discussion of both transferable features and pitfalls
Students transforming or translating short sentences
Reflection on how L1 knowledge informs English learning
non_examples: Focusing exclusively on L1 interference as deficit
Teaching rules in isolation without contrast

strategies.core_principle: Use structured peer interaction, allowing L1 discussion toward English output.
implementation_steps: Group same-L1 peers for comprehension in L1; require English output products.
Provide protocols and sentence frames; monitor and debrief.
source_docs: Bilingual Teaching Strategies Reference Guide - ChatGPT.docx
Bilingual Teaching Strategies Research Guide - Gemini.docx

definitions.short_en: Structured peer interaction that promotes negotiation of meaning, language practice, and co-construction of knowledge.
definitions.long_en: Collaborative Learning involves structured peer activities in which students jointly engage in tasks that require both academic thinking and language use. Typical formats include think‑pair‑share, jigsaw reading, expert groups, and project-based group work. Teachers set clear roles, provide sentence frames, and monitor to ensure equitable participation. Collaboration prompts students to negotiate meaning, clarify concepts, and use academic language functions such as justifying, comparing, or predicting. It supports both comprehension (listening to peers) and production (speaking/writing to contribute). Because peers often share linguistic resources, bilingual explanations can surface naturally and can be bridged to English output. Collaborative learning is powerful for engagement and oral language development but requires explicit norms; without structure, some students may dominate while others remain silent. Teachers must circulate, scaffold language, and hold groups accountable for both process and product.
look_fors: Students working in pairs or groups with clear roles
Use of sentence frames and word banks during discussion
Peer explanations and negotiation of meaning
Teacher monitoring and scaffolding group talk
non_examples: Unstructured group work with unclear goals
One student completing the task for the group
Silent groups working individually without interaction

strategies.core_principle: Use visuals, interactive charts, chants, and collaborative structures to teach content + language.
implementation_steps: Plan pictorial input/observation charts; co-construct sentence patterning charts.
Leverage chants and expert groups; revisit charts across the unit.
source_docs: Bilingual Teaching Strategies Reference Guide - ChatGPT.docx
Bilingual Teaching Strategies Research Guide - Gemini.docx

definitions.short_en: Guided Language Acquisition Design integrates thematic units, visuals, and cooperative structures to build language through content.
definitions.long_en: GLAD (Guided Language Acquisition Design) is a professional development model and framework that equips teachers with strategies to deliver content and language instruction simultaneously. GLAD emphasizes thematic unit design, high student engagement, and multimodal input. Signature practices include pictorial input charts, process grids, chants, and cooperative group structures that embed vocabulary and syntax in meaningful contexts. Students interact with visual, oral, and kinesthetic inputs, then produce language through structured output activities. GLAD is often implemented school-wide and pairs well with bilingual programs because it leverages visuals and cooperative learning to scaffold rigorous content. It should not be reduced to isolated tricks; fidelity involves unit-level planning and consistent use of the repertoire. When implemented fully, GLAD sustains both content mastery and robust language development.
look_fors: Use of pictorial input charts and process grids
Integration of visuals, oral input, and student output
non_examples: Overuse of chants without content tie-in
Using GLAD elements without unit planning
